name: Celery
what_it_does: Distributed task queue for Python. Executes background tasks asynchronously across worker processes, supports task scheduling (celery beat), retry logic, task chaining (chord, group, chain), and result backends (Redis, database). The standard Python solution for background processing, email sending, image processing, and long-running agent operations.
use_cases: Offload long-running LLM API calls to Celery workers to avoid HTTP timeout issues in Python agent web services
Schedule periodic agent maintenance tasks (data refresh, cache warming, report generation) with Celery Beat
Build distributed Python agent pipelines using Celery chains and chords for parallel/sequential task composition
Process high-volume agent tasks (embeddings, document parsing, API calls) across a worker pool for throughput
Implement reliable agent background processing with automatic retries, dead letter queues, and failure alerting
not_for: Real-time sub-second task execution — Celery has scheduling overhead; use asyncio or actor frameworks for <100ms latency
Simple single-server async tasks — Python's asyncio, ThreadPoolExecutor, or APScheduler are lighter for single-process use
Teams without Redis or RabbitMQ infrastructure — Celery requires a message broker as a hard dependency
best_when: You need distributed background task processing in Python with reliable delivery, retries, scheduling, and horizontal worker scaling.
avoid_when: You need sub-second latency or don't have a message broker — asyncio or arq (Redis-based lightweight queue) are better for simpler use cases.

auth.notes: Task queue library — broker (Redis/RabbitMQ) auth configured via broker URL. No application-level auth in Celery itself.

pricing.notes: BSD license. Community-maintained with broad commercial adoption.

performance.rate_limits: No rate limits in Celery itself. Worker concurrency configurable. Rate limiting per task queue available.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Task failure tracebacks stored in result backend. Worker log captures exceptions. Celery Flower dashboard provides visual task monitoring. Error messages in broker are JSON task metadata.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Tasks may execute more than once (at-least-once delivery). Use task.apply_async(task_id=...) with idempotency keys in task logic. acks_late=True for more reliable delivery.
known_agent_gotchas: Celery uses pickle serialization by default for task arguments — this is a security risk if broker is accessible from untrusted networks; always set task_serializer='json' in production
At-least-once delivery means tasks can execute multiple times on broker failures — agent tasks must be idempotent or use database-level deduplication
Celery 5.x requires Python 3.7+ and dropped Python 2 support — also changed default broker URL format; check migration guide when upgrading from Celery 4.x
Task results expire after 1 day by default (result_expires=86400) — long-running agent workflows that check results after 24 hours will find expired/missing results
Chord synchronization requires a result backend (Redis recommended) — chords fail silently without a configured result backend
Worker preforking model forks before loading your app — database connections (SQLAlchemy, Django ORM) must be re-established after fork using worker process lifecycle signals

security_score_components.security_notes: Use JSON serializer, not pickle. Broker URL contains credentials — use environment variables. Network isolation for broker is recommended. Worker processes run arbitrary Python code.
